findings.title: Hardcoded plaintext admin password in database seed data
findings.description: A hardcoded plaintext credential (username: 'admin', password: 'password123') is seeded directly into the SQLite database at application startup. This is the live working credential accepted by the /login endpoint. Any developer, auditor, or attacker with read access to the source file immediately has valid credentials. The value is not a placeholder or test fixture — it is the only user record in the database and the application accepts it for authentication.
findings.remediation: Remove the hardcoded credential from source code entirely. Seed credentials at deployment time using environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Before storing any password, hash it with bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2id (minimum work factor 12 for bcrypt). Never store or compare plaintext passwords.

findings.title: User credentials transmitted in plaintext via GET query parameters
findings.description: The /login route accepts credentials via HTTP GET query parameters (e.g., /login?username=admin&password=password123). Passwords in query strings are recorded in web server access logs, browser history, browser URL bars, Referer headers sent to third-party resources, and any intermediate proxy or CDN logs. This makes every authentication attempt a credential-logging event across multiple uncontrolled systems.
findings.remediation: Change the login route to HTTP POST and read credentials from the request body (req.body), not the query string. Use express.json() or express.urlencoded() middleware to parse the body. Ensure the connection is served exclusively over HTTPS to protect credentials in transit.

findings.title: User-supplied passwords logged to stdout via console.log
findings.secret_preview: Running query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '...' AND password = '...'
findings.full_context:
const query = `SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '${username}' AND password = '${password}'`;
console.log("Running query:", query);
findings.description: The full raw SQL query, including the user-submitted plaintext password interpolated directly into the string, is logged to stdout on every login attempt. In any production or development environment where stdout is captured (systemd journal, Docker logs, log aggregators such as Splunk/Datadog/ELK), every password submitted to the application is permanently recorded in plaintext. This also directly enables SQL injection because unsanitized user input is concatenated into the SQL string.
findings.scope: all login requests; credentials written to any stdout consumer
findings.remediation: Remove the console.log statement entirely. Never log credentials, authentication tokens, or query strings containing sensitive data. If query logging is needed for debugging, use parameterized queries (db.get('SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?', [username, password], ...)) so the SQL driver substitutes values internally and credentials are never part of a loggable string. Restrict debug logging behind a flag that is disabled in production.

findings.title: Passwords stored in plaintext in database — no hashing applied
findings.full_context:
db.run("CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER, username TEXT, password TEXT)");
db.run("INSERT INTO users VALUES (1, 'admin', 'password123')");
findings.description: The users table schema stores passwords in a plain TEXT column with no indication of hashing, salting, or any cryptographic transformation. The seed record confirms this: the literal string 'password123' is inserted and the login logic compares it directly against the query parameter value. A database read (via SQL injection, a backup leak, or direct file access) immediately yields all user passwords in recoverable form. Combined with SEC-001, the hardcoded value and the storage mechanism are both plaintext.
findings.remediation: Redesign the authentication flow to hash passwords before storage using a memory-hard algorithm such as Argon2id (preferred), bcrypt (cost factor ≥ 12), or scrypt. Use a library such as 'bcrypt' or 'argon2' for Node.js. On login, compare the submitted password against the stored hash using the library's constant-time comparison function (e.g., bcrypt.compare()). Never store or compare raw password strings. Migrate existing plaintext passwords by forcing a password reset for all affected accounts.

summary.scope_notes: Secrets scan with tool-assisted and manual review
